statusLine: Americas oil markets remain elevated above $98/bbl amid persistent Middle East shipping disruptions, while US crude inventories draw down sharply by 9.9 million barrels.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets are trading in elevated territory with WTI climbing 1.48% week-on-week to $98.92/bbl in the week ending May 22, 2026, maintaining pressure on American consumers already facing gasoline prices above $4.60/gallon. The significant 9.9 million barrel draw in commercial crude stocks signals tightening domestic supply conditions, while US production remains robust at 13.7 million barrels per day. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve sits at just 81.6 million barrels, representing minimal emergency buffer capacity.

The WTI-Brent spread has widened to -$6.90, reflecting the premium international markets are commanding due to severe shipping disruptions in critical maritime chokepoints. Multiple CRITICAL-level maritime advisories remain active for the Red Sea, Bab el Mandeb Strait, Persian Gulf, and Strait of Hormuz, where Houthi and Iranian attacks on commercial vessels are forcing costly route diversions and insurance premiums that ultimately impact global crude pricing.

Canada's heavy crude (WCS) likely faces continued pressure from the wide WTI-Brent differential, as pipeline constraints limit export capacity while international markets command higher premiums. Brazil's pre-salt production and Guyana's rapidly expanding Stabroek output provide some Atlantic Basin supply stability, though these volumes cannot fully offset Middle East disruptions affecting global tanker routes.

Retail fuel prices show mixed signals with gasoline essentially flat at $4.621/gallon but diesel declining more significantly by 4.3 cents to $5.596/gallon. The elevated crude prices and inventory draws suggest upward pressure on refined products may resume, particularly as summer driving season approaches across North America.
keyPoints: WTI crude rose 1.48% week-on-week to $98.92/bbl in the week ending May 22, maintaining elevated pricing pressure
US commercial crude inventories fell sharply by 9.9 million barrels, signaling tightening supply conditions
Critical maritime disruptions continue in Red Sea and Persian Gulf, with multiple MARAD advisories forcing costly shipping diversions
WTI-Brent spread widens to -$6.90, reflecting international market premiums from Middle East shipping risks
US gasoline prices remain elevated above $4.60/gallon despite minimal weekly change
Strategic Petroleum Reserve at just 81.6 million barrels provides limited emergency supply buffer
